critiques.context: Public landing/splash page. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Is the first impression striking enough to convert a PowerPoint user?

strengths: The copy (The last time presentations got an upgrade) is highly evocative and sells the problem/solution narrative well.
The dark theme with bright accent colors feels modern and visually sophisticated, aligning with the desired 'design studio' aesthetic.
The modal structure effectively focuses the user on a single path: continue or skip.

issues.description: Because this is only the splash page, it fails to communicate *how* familiar the editing process will be. The focus must immediately pivot after conversion to show layout elements (ribbons, side panels) that reassure existing power users.
The typography is clean but lacks strong character accents within the copy itself; relying too heavily on white text can feel flat despite the dark background.
While 'Continue' is a clear CTA, its design (a solid rectangular button) is generic. It needs more neon/vivid emphasis to pop against the modern aesthetic and increase urgency.

critique.top_suggestion: When designing the transition *past* this splash screen into the editor, ensure the primary visual anchor immediately reminds the user of standard MS Office layouts (e.g., showing the Ribbon Tabs or a clear slide thumbnail strip) so the 'modern' flair doesn't cause confusion about where to find familiar tools.

critiques.context: Login form. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Should feel clean and premium, not generic SaaS.

Signup/registration. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Is onboarding friction clear and minimal?

critiques.context: Password recovery. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Does it maintain the brand aesthetic even on a utility page?

strengths: The flow is incredibly clear and minimal, requiring zero cognitive load for password recovery.
The CTA button ('Send Reset Link') has high contrast and excellent visibility.
The typography usage (sans-serif) remains clean and modern.

issues.description: This page completely deviates from the stated 'dark ink background' brand aesthetic. Using a plain white utility modal undermines the entire premium, striking design studio vision.
While the component itself is clear, the lack of any visual branding (e.g., neon accents or sophisticated graphic elements) makes it feel generic and unaligned with the high-design goal.
The overall container feels very basic; integrating the 'Percy' brand identity visually into the utility page would help maintain consistency.

critique.top_suggestion: Apply the dark ink background, clean typography, and vivid/neon accent colors (the core branding elements) to all system-level pages (e.g., password recovery, account settings) to ensure visual consistency across all application states, regardless of functional necessity.

critiques.context: Main dashboard. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Does it feel like a modern design tool's home screen (Figma, Canva) while surfacing projects clearly?

strengths: Strong aesthetic commitment (dark mode/neon accents) immediately signals 'modern design tool.'
The underlying layout structure suggested by the background elements is clean and uses ample negative space.
Typography choices are highly readable and professional.

issues.description: The modal overlay completely obscures the core dashboard functionality. The initial user experience should lead with clear actions (e.g., 'New Presentation,' 'Open Project') rather than a lengthy marketing narrative.

While the aesthetics are modern, the sheer density of text in the onboarding/modal makes it feel less like an intuitive design tool and more like reading an article. The primary CTA ('CONTINUE') is too heavily weighted by explanatory text.

The background dashboard structure lacks clear visual hierarchy for projects. If this were a live view, differentiating between 'Active' vs. 'Archived' or assigning quick status indicators would improve usability.

critique.top_suggestion: Refactor the onboarding modal. Keep the compelling copy but make it highly scannable and significantly reduce its size. The immediate focus must be on getting the user to the central task (creating/opening a presentation), making the primary CTA ('New Presentation' or 'Start Designing') visible within 1-2 seconds of login.

critiques.context: Projects listing. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Does the grid/list of presentations feel PowerPoint-file-manager-like?

The lack of visible workflow components (ribbons, slide canvas structure) fails to prove the 'PowerPoint familiarity' promise."}, {"severity": "medium", "description": "The dramatic, essay-like copy ('PowerPoint replaced flipping through physical slides...') is overly verbose for a primary UI component and risks overwhelming the user before they even start working."}, {"severity": "low", "description": "While visually striking, the focus on 'upgrade' language could create undue anxiety or skepticism if not immediately followed by clear, simple UI examples."}],"top_suggestion": "Do not use this narrative style for core editor views. Use this aesthetic only in tooltips, modal dialogs, and initial onboarding screens, ensuring that when the user reaches the actual slide canvas, the full ribbon/toolbar structure (Home, Insert, Design tabs) is visible to ground them in familiar territory

critiques.context: User settings. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. Settings pages should still have the brand polish.

strengths: The use of clear section dividers (e.g., 'Appearance', 'Account') establishes good information hierarchy.
The dark mode palette with vivid accents successfully achieves the desired modern design studio feel.
The layout is clean and breathable, avoiding visual clutter even in a dense settings panel.

issues.description: The 'Appearance' section feels disjointed. It presents two distinct theme choices (Gatsby evening/letter) but doesn't offer a clear comparison or mechanism for applying them, making the user wonder which choice is default or recommended.

The CTAs and interactive elements lack consistent visual hierarchy. For example, 'Manage Members' and the dropdowns need stronger contrast against the dark background to feel actionable, preventing them from blending into mere text labels.

While modern, the overall structure relies heavily on plain text containers rather than clear card or pane separation. This could be slightly improved with subtle borders or shadows (even if dark) to help users delineate related groups of settings more effectively.

critique.top_suggestion: Consolidate configuration options within a section using distinct, self-contained cards for immediate clarity. For instance, when selecting 'Theme,' frame the two choices not just as text blocks but as interactive, selectable components with clear radio button or toggle states to guide the user's choice and show which theme is currently active.

critiques.context: The main slide editor — THE core UI. Percy aims to look like a super-modern, visually-popping design studio (dark ink background, clean typography, neon/vivid accents) but feel instantly familiar to any PowerPoint/Keynote user. CRITICAL: the canvas area and toolbar layout MUST mirror PowerPoint exactly (ribbon tabs top, slide strip left/bottom, properties panel right, white canvas center). Evaluate PowerPoint familiarity rigorously.
